coredata.dc:title: Layered double hydroxide-based photocatalysts for hydrogen production: Innovations, attributes and future prospects

coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic hydrogen (H2) production is a promising substitute for sustainable energy production. Considerable research has been engrossed in improving the ability of photocatalysts to harness solar energy and renovate it to chemical energy all around the globe. Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) were able to garner substantial scientific interest owing to their outstanding features, including the flexibility to exchange interlayer guest anions, tunable intralayer metal cations, and their capability for delamination. Their exclusive layered structure and the ability to modify the band gap make LDHs highly relevant in the field of photocatalysis. Recent progress has been made in developing LDHs-based photocatalyst nanocomposites, and this article discusses the latest engineering strategies employed by various researchers to enhance their efficiency in photocatalytic H2 production. The kinetics of LDHs as photocatalysts are meticulously examined, providing valuable insights into their performance. Additionally, the review emphasizes how modification strategies have improved the characteristics of LDHs and enabled the development of photocatalytic H2 during the past five years. Special attention is paid to their large-scale applications in terms of sustainable energy production and ecological redressing. Furthermore, prospects are discussed, offering strategic recommendations to guide the development and optimization of LDHs-based photocatalysts for enhanced efficiency and broader implementation in the field of photocatalysis.

row.entry: The flower-like NiAl-LDH microsphere's extensive surface area enables strong contact with Mn0·05Cd0·95S nanorods, enhancing H2 generation by accelerating electron flow through its core-shell structure.

The refined architecture enhances thermodynamic H2 evolution, offers abundant active sites for photocatalytic redox reactions, accelerates electron migration and separation, and lowers the surface potential.

The NiAl-LDH/gC3N4 nanocomposite's 2D/2D heterojunction, which promotes electron-hole separation in the presence of light and elevates electron conductivity inside the composite, is responsible for the elevated photocatalytic effectiveness.
Strong redox capabilities and effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs have been established by dual direct Z-scheme photocatalysts for H2 evolution
The p-n junction contact's intrinsic electric field greatly promotes charge movement and inhibits electron-hole recombination, which accelerates photocatalytic H2 development.

Through type-II and Z-scheme heterojunctions, Z-scheme charge-transfer dynamics in a p-n heterostructure successfully mimic natural photosynthesis, significantly impacting photoinduced exciton pair transport and improving optical and electrical characteristics.

The p–n heterojunction amongst Cu3P and NiCoP, combined with NiCoP's 3D nanoflower structure, is vital for boosting catalyst efficiency by improving photogenerated carrier separation and extending charge lifespan, thereby enhancing photocatalytic H2 production.
Enhancing photocatalytic H2 evolution, S-scheme heterojunctions with active P species boost active sites and accelerate electron-hole pair separation, making P-modification a key strategy.

Photocatalytic H2 evolution is enhanced by the S-scheme heterojunction composite catalyst, which has the best light absorption intensity along with photocurrent responsiveness, the smallest electronic resistance, and good electron and hole separation efficiency.

The formation of 0D p-type NiS species in situ formed a NiS/NiAl-LDH p–n heterojunction, enriching active sites and boosting photocatalytic H2 evolution by hastening charge carrier separation and migration.
The porous SCN structure facilitates strong NiCo-LDH binding, forming a Z-scheme heterojunction, and enhancing charge separation via reduced transport distance and IEF.
Remarkable efficiency in H2 evolution due to core structures' enhanced light absorption eventually improving charge migration
S-scheme served as an effective strategy to enhance the photocatalytic performance of a single wide-spectrum semiconductor.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The prominence of employing LDHs as co-catalysts in recent five years.

row.entry: H2 evolution has significantly increased reaching 4.6 times that of P–CdS.

CoSX/NiCo-LDH substantially broadens the absorption spectrum of CdS to visible light while simultaneously speeding up the efficient transfer of charges. A remarkable H2 evolution rate, 59 times that of the original CdS, was demonstrated by the composite photocatalyst.

Composite achieved 1.5 times higher H2 production efficiency due to the interfacial electric field formed by P-LDH's higher surface work function compared to g-C3N4, enhancing charge transfer, reducing photogenerated charge recombination, and lowering g-C3N4's surface overpotential.

14 times more H2 evolution than 2D-C3N4. To construct effective water-splitting photocatalysts, this investigation proposed a rational interface engineering technique for heterostructure creation with the right hole transport co-catalyst.

The NiS cocatalysts, with narrow bandgap, excellent light-harvesting ability, and ultrafine particle size, an active sites for the photocatalytic reaction, resulting in an H2 production rate 13.3 times higher than that of pure MgAl-LDHs.
Since the special embedded dispersed CDs had many active sites and encouraged charge separation, the photocatalyst was able to produce a high H2 value.
Ti3AlC2 in combination with CoAl-LDH/g-C3N4 boosted charge carrier separation.

Effective adherence of Ni1Co1Al1 LDH provides a close interface contact, therefore accelerating electron transfer to the LDH surface. By maximizing photon usage and encouraging more electron-hole pair formation.

head.ce:title: Layered double hydroxide-based photocatalysts for hydrogen production: Innovations, attributes and future prospects

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic hydrogen (H2) production is a promising substitute for sustainable energy production. Considerable research has been engrossed in improving the ability of photocatalysts to harness solar energy and renovate it to chemical energy all around the globe. Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) were able to garner substantial scientific interest owing to their outstanding features, including the flexibility to exchange interlayer guest anions, tunable intralayer metal cations, and their capability for delamination. Their exclusive layered structure and the ability to modify the band gap make LDHs highly relevant in the field of photocatalysis. Recent progress has been made in developing LDHs-based photocatalyst nanocomposites, and this article discusses the latest engineering strategies employed by various researchers to enhance their efficiency in photocatalytic H2 production. The kinetics of LDHs as photocatalysts are meticulously examined, providing valuable insights into their performance. Additionally, the review emphasizes how modification strategies have improved the characteristics of LDHs and enabled the development of photocatalytic H2 during the past five years. Special attention is paid to their large-scale applications in terms of sustainable energy production and ecological redressing. Furthermore, prospects are discussed, offering strategic recommendations to guide the development and optimization of LDHs-based photocatalysts for enhanced efficiency and broader implementation in the field of photocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Fossil fuels are the predominant source of energy worldwide, their reduction and scarcity have raised global apprehensions. Currently, it is estimated that 85 % of all energy consumed is derived from fossil fuels. The constant reliance on fossil fuels will destructively affect both ecology and economic development [1,2]. Hydrogen, one of the amplest elements on Earth, is looked upon as a more practical alternative to conventional fuels for meeting future energy demands [3]. H2, a fuel derived from solar energy, has a remarkable gravimetric heating value of 141.9 MJ/kg, surpassing other fuels like methanol (20 MJ/kg), diesel (44.8 MJ/kg), gasoline (47.5 MJ/kg), and methane (55.5 MJ/kg). H2 also has low CO2 emissions and supports electricity generation. Furthermore, the energy released during H2 combustion is approximately 122 kJ/mol, nearly 2.5 times greater per weight than conventional fuels. [4,5]. Commercial H2 production methods which include steam reforming, coal gasification, along catalytic partial oxidation depend on the reforming of fossil fuels, which raises several ecological issues. In addition, water requires exceptionally high temperatures (∼2100 K) for its thermal breakdown. Therefore, there is an immediate hunger for cost-effective, sustainable, and durable resources [6]. Photocatalysis has acquired immense attention over the past few decades. Previous studies have shown that H2 production via photocatalytic water splitting results in minimal air pollution, no global warming, and upfront storage. Consequently, this method is considered the utmost capable future energy source owing to its sustainability, sanitation, and renewability, with a lack of toxins. Notably, a suitable photocatalyst can efficiently facilitate the photocatalysis process under sunlight. Unfortunately, the majority of photocatalysts are restricted in their ability to absorb solar energy since they are only active in the UV range, which makes up only 3–4 % of the total solar spectrum. Therefore, to achieve the 7th and 13th goals of the UN, which are "Affordable and Clean Energy" and "Climate Action," it is imperative to build photocatalytic devices that can capture visible light or near-infrared regions. This emphasizes how crucial it is to research photocatalysts. The field of photocatalysis is becoming increasingly recognized due to its multiple developments, which could eventually result in commercial applications [7].

The success of photocatalysis has led to the development of multiple visible light-active materials [8]. Nevertheless, for large-scale H2 production, the activity and photostability of these photocatalysts which include TiO2, CuO, ZnO, IrO2, WO3, and CdS are limited. Scarcity, high cost, low quantum efficiency, and a wide band gap are some of the primary factors influencing their availability [9–11]. Cds has been recognized as an exceptional photocatalyst owing to their elevated surface crystallinity and excellent photochemical characteristics. Nevertheless, its efficacy as a standalone photocatalyst is hindered by substantial photo-corrosion as a consequence of sulfide component's oxidation. A similar observation applies to TiO2, an extensively studies photocatalyst. TiO2 is valuable in photocatalysis owing to its exclusive features, abundance, and robust chemical stability [12]. While TiO2 exhibits extraordinary chemical properties, it still faces barriers because of the rapid recombination of photo-generated charges. Essential modifications are required to enhance its efficiency under solar radiation [13]. A diverse variety of semiconductors offer discrete advantages; for example, CuO and ZnO are widely opted for photocatalysis as they are cost-effective, safe, and abundant. However, for TiO2, there are restrictions such as limited light absorption, wide band gaps, and rapid electron-hole recombination. These materials are frequently combined with other semiconductors to create heterojunctions for enhanced charge separation [14,15].

Layered Double Hydroxides (LDHs), an extensively investigated group of photocatalysts, are frequently employed for their outstanding photocatalytic performance. Also known as hydrotalcite-like compounds, LDHs comprise of an edge-connected M(OH)6 octahedra with anions positioned among the hydrated interlayer galleries of the positively charged brucite-like host layers. In a nutshell, the general molecular formula is [M1∼x 2+ Mx3+(OH)2(An−)x/n] x+.mH2O, where M2+ and M3+ are trivalent metal cations (e.g., Al3+, Fe3+, Ga3+, Mn3+) and bivalent metal cations (e.g., Mg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+), respectively, and An-is the counter ion (e.g., CO3 2−, Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, PO4 3−) [16]. In LDHs, the metal octahedra is extensively linked by a metal-oxygen-metal oxo bridging-like structure. Restricting the redundant electron-holes recombination, these oxo bridges encourage visible light redox developments by enabling metal-to-metal charge transfer [17,18]. Additionally, hydroxyl radicals (OH), vital intermediates in oxidation reactions throughout the photocatalysis, can be engendered when O–H bonds among the coordination octahedron align perpendicularly to the brucite layers and intermingle with valence band (VB) holes. Hence, besides their flexible band gap, exclusive layered structure, large surface area, affordability, and natural availability, LDHs exhibit excellent physiochemical and photocatalytic characteristics, assuring a reliable strategy for H2 production [19]. Several studies have been conducted over the years aimed at improving the efficiency of photocatalysts based on LDHs for the production of H2. These efforts comprise modifying the morphology of LDHs, incorporating plasmonic materials, developing hybrid nanocomposites, and heterojunction formation with another photocatalyst that is active under visible light.

In the past five years, various reviews and research studies have been published, revealing comprehensive insights into various aspects of LDHs-based photocatalysts, chiefly their application in H2 production. A thorough study of LDHs-based photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants was published in 2020 by Zhang et al., who also projected the relevance of the mechanics behind the photocatalysis process. The review extensively examined the configuration of various LDHs-based photocatalysts and their ability to degrade several organic contaminants [19]. In 2022, Hanane Boumeriamne and colleagues published a paper highlighting the latest developments in LDHs-based nanoparticles for photocatalytic water splitting [20]. The review discusses the several artificial techniques and post-modifications used to improve their functionality. It investigated the effects of important parameters such as irradiation sources, cocatalysts, sacrificial agents, and catalyst loading and presented a thorough description of the experimental settings essential to achieve stronger photoactivity. The evaluation acts as a thorough manual for deciding which procedures will maximize the photoactivity of LDHs along with modified LDH-based products [20]. Azmat Ali Khan investigated the development of trimetallic and bimetallic LDHs in 2023 in a comprehensive review, as well as methods for synthesizing photocatalysts with high efficiencies. It went into additional details about the modifications made possible by embracing, mixing, and adding various combinations of cations and anions. The stability of LDH-based photocatalysts was thoroughly investigated, as were the structural alterations that occurred during the in-situ synthesis of LDHs. Additionally, it explored the potential uses of LDHs shortly for ecological and energy applications, emphasizing the improvements in characteristics of LDHs that were done via a variety of modification techniques [21]. However, throughout the previous two or three years, not many review articles that exclusively address developments in photocatalytic H2 production using LDHs-based photocatalysts have been published. This review article specifically highlights the importance of LDHs-based photocatalysts for H2 production, distinguishing it from existing reviews that primarily focus on the broader applications of LDHs. It begins with a concise overview of LDHs materials, followed by an analysis of their kinetics in photocatalytic water-splitting progressions. The article emphasizes the structural and electronic properties of LDHs-based photocatalysts and explores how these properties have been utilized by recent research to enhance photocatalytic H2 evolution. Additionally, it addresses the challenges of scaling up LDHs-based photocatalysts, while discussing their limitations. The review presents data from the most recent five years, providing a comprehensive update on the latest advancements in the field.

As demonstrated in Fig. 1 , Layered Double Hydroxides (LDHs) are a category of 2D anionic clay with a brucite-like structure. The general chemical formula for LDH is M12+M23+(OH)2ˣ+ (An−)x/n∙mH2O, where M12+ represents divalent metal cations such as Zn2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Co2+, etc.; M23+ includes trivalent metal cations like Cr3+, Al3+, Fe3+, Mn3+, among others; and An− denotes interlayer anions like CO3 2−, NO3 −, Cl−, SO4 2−, etc. The molar ratio of M3+/(M2+ + M3+), or x, typically falls between 0.2 and 0.33, whereas m denotes the quantity of water molecules in the interlayer region.

In LDHs, the metal cation can be surrounded by six OH− ions, generating an octahedral MO6 structural unit. A continuous layer is formed along the ab plane by the connections between these units at their edges. The piling of these layers occurs in an orderly manner along the c-axis, influenced by the presence of interlayer species. In certain circumstances, M2+ cations can be replaced by M3+ cations that have identical ionic radii, giving the host layers a partial positive charge. These layers' charge density rises in tandem with the molar (x) spike. Furthermore, the hydroxyl groups oriented vertically form H2 bonds with interlayer water molecules or anions, contributing to the distinguishing delamination properties of LDHs [22]. Factors such as the arrangements of intercalated anions, spacing among layers, and the type of valence metal cations, deeply influence the structure of LDHs [23]. The chemical characteristics of LDHs are significantly influenced by the interlayer spacing. The robust interactions among intercalated anions and metal hydroxide layers impede anion mobility in densely packed layers, which possibly reduces their reactivity. In contrast, amplified interlayer spacing enables greater anion mobility and interaction with diverse species, thereby enhancing reactivity. Moreover, the type of metal cations existing is also critical in determining the chemical conduct of LDHs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fundamentals of LDHs-based photocatalyst in water splitting
ce:section.ce:para: The semiconductor's physiochemical properties exert an essential effect on the photocatalytic water-splitting efficiency. For instance, a semiconductor's band gap is essential since it determines its ability to absorb light and facilitate precise reactions. Furthermore, the structure of a photocatalyst profoundly affects its surface area, which influences the adsorption of reactants and yield products. Integrating various semiconductors can lead to a synergistic effect, promoting reactions that cannot be attained via a single semiconductor. Therefore, employing well-optimized techniques to enhance photocatalytic activity has become crucial. Researchers are progressively drawn towards the exclusive morphological structure and properties of LDHs, prompting more exploration into the photocatalytic potential of hydrotalcite-based compounds. Possessing compositional versatility and flexible assembly [24].

LDHs typically consist of two layers piled on top of one another. The positively charged layer is termed as the “Brucite layer”, where as “interlamellar space” is the negatively charged layer. Anions are intercalated among the interlamellar region and the brucite layer is embellished with metal cations in an LDHs framework [25]. Its imperatively noteworthy that LDHs in their natural state have carbonate anions among the interlayer gallery. These anions can be substituted with a variety of different active species, including organic complexes, inorganic anions, and organic bases or acids [26]. Metal cations present within the brucite layer can be tailored with various other pairs of cations. The physiochemical properties of LDHs are profoundly influenced by the category and identity of these cations. The metal choice in an LDHs’ structure is vital since diverse metal combinations result in varying band gap values, producing LDHs with its novel tunable band gap characteristic [19]. In general, the metal cations are bonded to the OH− groups in LDHs via Van der Waals and electrostatic forces, whereas anions present in the interlayer are bonded to water molecules and H2 bonds with the brucite layer. These bonds help stabilize the framework of LDHs. Exhibiting advantageous properties LDHs have garnered significant interest as a visible light-active H2-producing photocatalyst. For an LDHs-based photocatalyst it is mandatory to meet standard criteria to effectively engage in redox reactions and produce H2, 1) the valence band (VB) should surpass the water oxidation potential 1.23 eV, 2) the conduction band (CB) potential should be greater than the reduction potential(>0.41 eV), 3) overall Gibbs free energy for water splitting ought to be less than the band gap energy of LDHs [27,28]. Details are provided in the following sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamics of LDHs-based photocatalyst for photocatalytic H2
ce:section.ce:para: LDHs-based photocatalyst has a similar kinetics to a convectional photocatalyst as depicted in Fig. 2 . The catalytic H2 production process involves four key stages: 1) adsorption of reactants, 2) generation and separation of charge carriers, 3) transport of photogenerated electrons and holes across interfaces, and 4) surface catalysis, as illustrated in the figure [29].

The absorptions of reactants mark the initial step of photocatalytic H2 production. During the process, the photocatalyst interacting with the reactant, functions efficiently without the necessity of a concentration gradient. The photocatalytic efficacy can be substantially enhanced by boosting the photocatalyst's definite surface area. Furthermore, employing diverse co-catalyst and sacrificial agents is crucial for effectual H2 evolution. The substantial surface area and abundance of surface basic sites depict LDHs-based photocatalysts as an excellent choice for promoting healthy photocatalytic activity.

Formation and separation of charge carriers are carried out in the second stage. Electrons upon absorbing the light energy higher than the bandgap energy (Eg) become over-excited and migrate from the VB towards the conduction band leaving holes behind. The bandgap of semiconductors plays a pivotal role throughout the procedure. Various studies have concluded that the bandgap ranging between 1.5 and 3.2 eV is considered idyllic for productive photocatalysis. The bandgap must exceed 1.5 eV to ensure a strong redox reaction and productive photocatalysis.
