The third phase emphasizes the movement of photo-generated carriers from within the semiconductor to its surface. The recombination of these carriers with holes, which lowers photocatalytic effectiveness, is a significant issue throughout this process. Researchers have developed S-scheme and Z-scheme heterojunctions to mitigate the impact of photocarrier recombination. These systems optimize overall performance by facilitating the spatial separation of photo-generated electrons and holes at the interface among two semiconductors. LDHs exhibiting and innovative band structure along with the presence of intralayer metal cations enables the establishment of a wide range of interfaces and heterostructures that facilitate carrier transport and inhibit recombination.

The surface catalytic reaction is the fourth step. In light of their large number of active sites and high specific surface area, LDHs are highly effective at this point. LDHs have more reactive sites and larger specific surface areas since they have surface OH groups and layered structures like traditional photocatalysts. These characteristics enhance reactant adsorption and surface catalytic reactions in the early stages. Furthermore, defect sites can be built into single-layer LDHs nanostructures to increase surface catalytic efficiency substantially.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modified synthesis of LDHs-based photocatalyst in recent years
ce:section.ce:para: Several articles have extensively covered traditional LDHs photocatalyst synthesis techniques, such as the Co-precipitation method, Hydrothermal method, Ion exchange method, and Sol-Gel method. Table 1 provides a deep insight into these technologies along with their pros and cons and Fig. 3 provides their schematic illustration for a better concept. In this section, we will focus on deliberating novel and advanced modifications to these synthesis techniques, highlighting their potential to enhance the performance and efficiency of LDHs-based photocatalysts.

As technology advances it was studied that the conventional methods are increasingly being supplemented with additional synthesis techniques for preparing modified novel LDHs photocatalysts. For example, integrating auxiliary methods such as microwave method, sulphuration, or ultrasonic radiation can enhance synthesis efficiency and improve structural ordering. In 2019, Songsong L. and colleagues [30] developed an innovative NiCo-LDH/P–CdS hybrid photocatalyst by integrating P-doping with in-situ NiCo-LDH loading techniques. P-doping was introduced to create a mid-gap close to the bottom of the Cds conduction band, prolonging the lifespan of photo-generated electrons. Consequently, the formation of heterojunctions with P-Cds is rendered by the in-situ loading of NiCo-LDH, which effectively lowers the light-induced deterioration often observed in Cds and improves carrier separation efficiency. The photocatalyst exhibited an H2 evolution rate of 8.665 mmol h−1 g−1. This study reported an innovative strategy for employing an eco-friendly and scalable approach to synthesize highly effective catalysts for H2 production. This method was able to attain robust synergistic effects by simultaneously refining and optimizing both surface and electronic structure. In 2019, Dandan et al. were able to successfully synthesize g-C3N4/ZnTi/LDHs by employing and upfront one-step in-situ hydrothermal approach. This fabrication method assisted in achieving a notable H2 production rate of 161.87 mmol h−1g−1. The mesoporous g-C3N4/ZnTi/LDHs-laminated van der Waals heterojunctions established in this study provided valuable insights into designing high-efficiency materials with van der Waals heterojunctions [31]. Jingshuai Chen, in 2020 reported the development of an innovative MgAl-LDH/NiS hybrid photocatalyst through a twos-tep strategy involving hydrothermal and precipitation methods. By increasing visible light absorption and promoting the exchange of charges, the incorporation of NiS substantially boosted the photocatalytic capability of MgAl-LDH, resulting in an H2 progression rate of 35.8 μmol. h−1g−1. The study demonstrated how inexpensive metal sulfide modifications can enhance photocatalytic activity that generates a considerable quantity of H2 when exposed to visible light [32]. Dipti Parva and colleagues, 2020, employed an in-situ co-precipitation technique to synthesize a novel S, P co-doped g-C3N4/ZnCr-LDH. The developed heterostructure revealed an enhanced photocatalytic activity by simultaneously degrading 95 % of ciprofloxacin in 90 min and achieving H2 production rate of 1319 μmol h−2. This practice proved to be a promising approach for effectively degrading ciprofloxacin and producing photocatalytic H2 [33]. An innovative ternary-shelled nanotubes were designed by Shuang Zhao et al., in 2021. These nanosheets were synthesized by in-situ loading on Ni–Fe LDHs that were originally derived from MIL-88A, revealing remarkable potential in photocatalytic applications. Within visible light, the reported ZIS@Ni–Fe/LDHs demonstrated an improved H2 yield of 2035.81 μmol h−1 g−1 [34]. Additionally, it possessed an established hollow structure and a substantial surface area. This design provided an efficient method to generate sophisticated heterojunction photocatalysts by maximizing light harvesting, reactive site availability, and interfacial electron transfer. Mohsen S. Mostafa and colleagues [35] in 2022 developed a novel 1D CoBiTi/LDHs with an upfront facile synthesis method, achieving a H2 evolution of 272.8 μmol g−1 h−1 without the need for scavengers. This efficiency is attributed to its low bandgap of 2.4 eV, surface defects, and interlayered (–CN) groups. The use of hybridized structures in this technique boosts H2 production and holds potential for commercial H2 production, contributing to efforts to mitigate the severe impacts of climate change for future generations. Jing Xu [36] developed a NiCo-LDH/CeO2 catalyst through in situ loading, which demonstrated excellent activity under simulated sunlight. The catalyst was tested under visible light irradiation and attained an H2 production efficiency of 2.7 mmol h−1 g−1. Its strong performance in interfacial charge transfer significantly enhances its photocatalytic activity. In 2022 Using urea hydrolysis to alter the precipitation of TiCl4 vapors, Mohsen S.at el. developed TCT-LDH and TiO2@TCT-LDH. Under infrared radiation, TCT-LDH managed to obtain an H2 evolution rate of 139 μmolg−1h−1 without requiring scavengers. In the meantime, without Pt-loading, the TiO2@TCT-LDH heterojunction achieved one of the highest H2 values yet recorded, at 2158 μmol g−1h−1. When [TiO3] sites are added to the LDH structure, a heterojunction with improved charge separation and mobility is produced. The novel-developed TiO2@TCT-LDH presents a viable method for clean, continuous H2 generation. An innovative 3D hierarchical CdS/NiAl-LDH photocatalyst was developed in 2023 by Gaimei et al. [37]. This catalyst was made of one-dimensional CdS nanorods and 3D, flower-shaped NiAl-LDH microspheres that were assembled using an elementary electrostatic technique. Successfully, an ideal H2 evolution rate of 3.24 μmol g−1h−1. Owing to the strong interface connection between CdS and NiAl-LDH and the integrated electric field, photo-generated charges were able to separate and migrate quickly, which significantly increased the production of H2. The reported novel 1D/3D interface design proved to be a valuable and cost-effective method for producing metal-free photocatalysts for H2 production. Bocho Mao developed ZnO@ZnS@NiAl-LDH in 2023. An in-situ synthesis technique was employed to synthesize ZnO@ZnS, which were used to build covalent connections at the interfaces. The positive NiAl-LDH was formed on the ZnO@ZnS surface by electrostatic self-assembly as a consequence of negatively charged ZnS. The maximum H2 production rate of 866.35 μmol g−1 h−1 was accomplished by composite [38]. Shaochun et al. employed a two-step hydrothermal approach to successfully synthesize NiCo-LDHs/CdSe (NCCSe) binary heterojunctions. The highest photocatalytic H2 production rate displayed by the composite was 2929.4 μmol g−1 h−1. This reported study was a framework that could be introduced to various LDHs materials for the development of S-type heterojunction based on NiCo-LDHs for effective photocatalytic H2 production [39]. In 2024 Liu et al. reported an upfront in-situ modification approach to integrate CeO2 with hollow NiCo-LDH nanosheets, developing an innovative NiCo-LDH/CeO2 nanosheets heterostructure catalyst with effective photocatalytic H2 production activity. The dye-sensitized NiCo-LDH/CeO2 nanosheets accomplished an H2 production rate of 4312 μmol g−1 h−1 under visible light irradiations This study provides new insights into the rational design of advanced photocatalysts [40].

Table 2 presents a detailed overview in tabular format, showcasing novel synthesis techniques of LDHs-based photocatalysts for H2 production in the past five years. It is evident from the table that throughout the years, hydrothermal and co-precipitation methods have experienced significant changes to elevate the structural, morphological, and photocatalytic properties of synthetic materials. Introducing various kinds of optimizations, such as the doping of other materials into the base catalyst or the functionalization of the surface, and even the hybridization material respectively, has been implemented to increase efficiency and safety. These innovations illustrate the concept of synthesis techniques as constantly changing and leading the way for more efficient and sustainable photocatalysts.

LDHs have unique and robust structural and electronic properties that enhance their effectiveness as a photocatalyst. Fig. 4 demonstrates the visual representation of electronic and structural properties. Details of how these properties act in boosting the H2 are as follows.

Anions, indicated as An-, are exchangeable species that serve to control the cations' positive charge and exist within the interlayer gaps of LDHs materials. LDHs structures' cationic layers may alter the interlayer spacing to accommodate a broad spectrum of anions as there is no crosslinking among them. Anions primarily facilitate charge carrier conduction, resulting in the production of electron/hole pairs that are required in photocatalytic activity. The anionic guest species within the LDHs interlayer region are reimbursed for any charge deficiencies, ensuring electrical neutrality. The anions present between anions can significantly modify the surface electrical characteristics and enhance the specific surface area of LDHs. Moreover, these interlayer ions can influence the material's capability to absorb reactants [73]. Anions play a vital role in the stability of LDHs-based photocatalysts. The addition of several anions to enhance the steadiness of photocatalysts has been widely studied. Additions of anionic charges lead to stable LDHs structures by strengthening the electrostatic interactions among the anions and the positively charged OH layers [74]. Carbonate-based-interlayer LDHs typically exhibit better stability than Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2− anion-based composites. A study by Sasai et al. indicated that LDHs having carbonate ions demonstrate much better stability within their respective regions [75]. The anion exchange capacity enables researchers to embed different anionic functionalities into the interlayer region of LDHs materials. The interlayer anions modify LDH's bandwidth gap and surface behavior while altering its electronic structure for enhanced photocatalytic H2 production. Certain anions including sulfates and phosphates function as electron acceptors and donors which promotes the split of electron-hole pairs (e−/h+) after photoexcitation [76]. The reduced rates of recombination along with increased electron availability support water reduction processes for H2 generation.

LDHs exhibit an appealing feature known as the “reconstruction effect” also termed as “reconstitution effect”. High-temperature calcination will disrupt the layered structure of the LDHs material and turn it into Layered Double Oxides (LDO); but, upon rehydration, the LDO will return to its original layered structure. When recreating its structure, the memory effect of LDHs may result in a significant number of oxygen vacancies on the surface which is beneficial for the stability of overall photocatalytic activity [77]. LDHs calcined at a specific temperature can reabsorb various anions and water molecules, enabling them to rehydrate and revert to their initial layered structure owning to this effect [78]. Wen et al. reported an innovative study on ultrathin ZnGa-BLDH-CR by employing the “memory effect” of hydrotalcite. Borate anions were incorporated into the interlayers to restore their layered morphology. The developed ZnGa-BLDH-CR displayed H2 evolution activity pf 160 μmol g−1 h−1. Using a deliberate approach, this study formed an ultra-thin 2D LDHs nanosheet along with a dye-sensitized photocatalytic reaction that concurrently generates H2 and degrades dyes, efficiently utilizing waste water for H2 production while purifying the water [79]. LDHs generate new active sites together with defects on their surface during the reconstruction process. These sites along with new active sites and defects strengthen the capability of reactant absorption (for instance water molecules) while boosting photocatalytic performance. Restoring LDHs layers through the memory effect enables better photogenerated electron-hole pair (e−/h+) transmission and mobility due to the structured material design. The improved charge carrier availability supports redox reactions particularly water splitting for H2 production since recombination rates reduce during this process. The capability of LDHs to restore their structural composition through the memory effect enables them to function as sustainable and reusable photocatalysts. Relying on the memory effect enables reduced catalyst replacement requirements which decreases operational costs in practical applications [80].

LDHs consist of metal cations arranged within octahedral brucite layers and encircled by hydroxyl groups and interlayer anions. They are widely recognized as capable photocatalysts due to their high redox action, eco-friendliness, and operative incorporation of homogeneously distributed transition metal atoms [81]. The metal cations within the brucite layers of LDHs can be revised via numerous cation combinations, prompting the optical and chemical characteristics of the material. The optimal metal present within the LDHs structure has remarkable tunability and is essential in establishing the band gap [19]. In LDHs morphology, metal cations typically bound to hydroxide groups via electrostatic interactions and Van der Waals forces. Anions among the interlayer, linked with water molecules, contribute significantly to the structural stability by the linking of the brucite layer through H2 bonding [82].

The choice of metal cations (Mg2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Al3+, Fe3+, and Cr3+) determines both the electronic structure and bandgap of LDHs. The bandgap adjustment through metal cation composition control enables enhanced solar energy harvesting in either visible or UV wavelengths for H2 production systems. Photo-generated electron-hole pairs (e−/h+) undergo more effective separation when specific metal cations are integrated. Transition metal cations Ni2+ and Co2+ function as electron traps with in an LDHs to boost H2 production efficiency through reduced electron recombination [80]. Ni2+ Co2+ and Fe3+ function as active sites for the H2 evolution reaction when used as metal cations. The water molecule activation and adsorption process becomes more efficient through cation addition which improves the overall photocatalytic activity.

The doping modification scheme involves the adjustment of metal cations or anions into the fundamental layers of LDHs material to augment their properties. This strategy is an upfront approach to convert binary metal LDHs into ternary or quaternary forms by simply introducing the opted doping metal anion or cations. Cation doping initiates the synergistic interaction among metal elements, such as Fe and Co, enhancing the LDHs’ electron transfer ability. Moreover, it accelerates the evolution of Co from a valence state of +3 to +2, thereby improving catalytic efficiency [83]. Cation-doped LDHs enhance the surface area by expanding the host layer and increasing the number of anions within the guest layer [84].

Strategic dopant incorporation into LDHs has demonstrated them as effective photocatalysts for H2 production. The incorporation of transition metals like Ni, Co, Fe, Cu, or non-metals including N, S, and P into LDHs structures yields modifications to electronic properties along with bandgap and surface chemistry characteristics [85]. The introduction of dopants produces energy levels inside the band structure that enhance light absorption across the visible spectrum while minimizing photogenerated e−/h+ pair recombination [21]. It was observed that the addition of Ni2+ and Co2+ dopants in LDHs endorses active components that enhance water molecule reduction processes whereas nitrogen-based dopants boost both electrical conduction and carrier movement capabilities [9]. LDHs lattices accept dopants that produce defective sites and vacancies which boost surface reactivity plus photocatalytic performance. Through optimal dopant selection and careful optimization, researchers can transform LDHs into more efficient sustainable energy photocatalysts by boosting their H2 evolution rates.

Charge transfer at the interfaces inside an LDHs-based hybrid semiconductor escalates the separation of electron-hole pairs, minimizes the carrier recombination, and stretches out the carrier lifetimes ultimately improving the photocatalytic efficiency [86,87]. The heterojunction is generally defined as the interface between two distinctive semiconductors with distinct band structures with aligned band gaps [88]. LDHs have exhibited remarkable potential in photocatalytic applications, however employing them as a standalone photocatalyst offers various challenges. Their restricted photochemical energy and speedy recombination of charge carriers hamper their efficiency in photocatalytic H2 production. Heterojunction formation is a leading operational strategy for tackling these challenges by improving the mobility and separation of photo-generated carriers and enhancing the optical absorption spectrum. A notable improvement in photocatalytic effectiveness becomes apparent when single-component LDHs are coupled with different semiconductors of the appropriate band through synergistic interactions. Semiconductor interfaces are typically categorized into different types including type I, type II, type III, Z-scheme, S-scheme, p-n junction, and R-scheme. These heterojunction systems are generally distinguished based on explicit features such as (1) band alignment (2) specific semiconductor used and (3) the process of electron transport. When it comes to band alignment, the type II heterojunction demonstrates an outstanding band structure, where the valence band (VB) of the oxidation photocatalyst and the conduction band (CB) of the reduction photocatalyst are situated at lower band potentials [89]. Respectively, for a redox reaction to transpire, holes migrate from a higher to a lower VB, while electrons travel from a higher to lower CB potential. The band structure and configuration of the p-n junction are therefore alike to those found in type I [90]. However, the S and Z-scheme's band potential arrangement differs from type II and p-n junctions'. In these arrangements, the VB of the reduction catalyst is at a lower potential than the VB of the oxidation catalyst, but the CB of the reduction catalyst is at a greater potential than the oxidation catalyst. Transferring electrons from an inferior CB potential to a higher one facilitates the reduction process. Linking conductors form the p-n junction, where p-type semiconductors serve as an oxidation photocatalyst. On the other hand, the S and Z schemes usually use n-type semiconductors to facilitate reduction and oxidation. Additionally, an inert internal electric field developed in these systems by the development of positively and negatively charged surfaces promotes the segregation of photo-generated charges [91].

To achieve operative interfacial contact, Yijin Wang and associates synthesized a type II heterojunction photocatalyst through the combination of cobalt oxide (CoO) and nickel-cobalt LDHs (NiCo-LDH) with cobalt atom bridges. Through the integration of 1D CoO nanowires with appropriately aligned CoO and NiCo-LDH energy levels, effective charge carrier separation and transfer at the heterojunction interfaces were enabled. The design also advanced to form an increased surface area, and optimized structure, boosting visible light absorption. The CoO/NiCo-LDH heterojunction demonstrated an impressive photocatalytic H2 generation, achieving ranges up to 1.5 mmol g−1 h−1 and maintaining 96 % of its initial efficiency after 480 min of continuous operation, as a result of these compositional and structural adjustments [92]. Cao Yue and colleagues synthesized a p-n heterojunction, introducing a 2D/3D architecture by a combination of rock-like CuI with CoAl-LDH nanosheets. This novel assembly enhances the p-n heterojunction among CuI and CoAl-LDH, pointedly enhancing simultaneously the optical and electrochemical attributes of the material. These progressions led to enhanced photo-generated carrier separation and transport, ultimately delivering a maximal H2 progression of 3.59 mmol g−1 h−1. CuI, in this study, functions as an active hole acceptor with p-n heterojunction for boosted H2 generation [93]. Correspondingly, In a comparable study, an internal electric field (IEF)-driven S-scheme system described by Tao et al. was synthesized with 2D molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) and CoAl-LDH. The augmented photocatalytic recital was accredited to the hasty separation of electron pairs and the robust redox behavior of the MoS2/CoAl-LDH hybrid, achieved via the S-scheme transfer mechanism permitted by IEF. In addition to this, improved H∗ absorption and desirable 2D structure contributed to elevated water reduction performance, offering a novel strategy to employ IEF-induced S-scheme photocatalyst with 2D assembly [51].

Ping et al. reported the synthesis of ZnCds nanoparticles encapsulated within NiCo-LDHs supplemented with oxygen vacancies (ZCS@LDH-Ov), which demonstrated a strong IEF and vigorous interfacial bonding. The ZCS@LDH-Ov composite revealed a remarkable photocatalytic H2 evolution of 2.68 mmol g−1 h−1. The respective Z-scheme is responsible for enhancing the H2 evolution activity by promoting rapid charge transfer and separation. Additionally, the excellent recyclability of ZCS@LDH-Ov is attributed to the improved photo-corrosion resistance of ZnCdS, facilitated by the effective transfer of photo-generated holes through a fast interfacial charge channel [94]. Table 3 provides some of the reported LDHs-based photocatalysts in the last five years.

2D nanostructured materials offer substantial features such as excellent charge transfer rates, large specific surface areas, plentiful active sites, and exceptional responsiveness to visible light. Consequently, compared to conventional materials, 2D nanostructured materials like LDHs exhibit significantly improved photocatalytic performance.

The alteration of its nanostructure invariably brings up certain defects, such as cation and anion vacancies [107], hence significantly improving the reactant's adsorption and activation. A cation vacancy forms when a positive ion fails to occupy its proper crystal lattice position. The gap represents an open space where an essential cation should exist thus disrupting the structural ionic stability. Similarly, the absence of a negatively charged (anionic) ion creates an anion vacancy since it fails to occupy its regular crystal lattice position. A void is formed between the atoms of the lattice structure. The term Oxygen Vacancy defines a particular type of anion vacancy that develops because of missing oxygen ion positions in oxide crystal lattices. Vacant positions in the material structure modify the properties by shaping both electrical conduction and chemical activity characteristics [108]. The presence of defects improves the adsorption and activation mechanisms by constraining the distribution of surface charge density in the photocatalyst, thus reducing the energy barrier for the photocatalytic reaction [109].

Bai et al. [94] developed ZnCdS nanoparticles encased in NiCo-LDHs with enriched oxygen vacancies (ZCS@LDH-Ov), which exhibited an enhanced internal electric field and strong interfacial binding. In its synthesized form, ZCS@LDH-Ov achieved an impressive photocatalytic H2 generation rate of 2.68 mmol g−1 h−1. Zeta et al. reported that the electrostatic attraction between ZnIn2S4 nanosheets and CoAl-LDHs led to the formation of a unique ultrathin 2D/2D S-scheme heterostructure. The presence of oxygen vacancies in the monolayer CoAl-LDHs nanosheet smooths the formation of Co-SX bonds, which assist as charge transfer channels at the CoAl-LDHs/ZnIn2S4 interface. This structure achieves a maximum photocatalytic H2 evolution activity of 1563.64 μmol g−1 h−1, credited to the ultrathin structure's shorter carrier paths, rapid photogenerated charge transfer, and the efficient S-scheme migration pathway enabled by the Co-SX charge channels [110].

Lixu et al. synthesized CoAl-LDH/Carbon Nitride with carbon vacancies heterojunction by employing an electrostatic self-assembly technique. H2 evolution activity of 2515 μmol g−1 in 5 h was observed. An effective charge transfer and carrier separation were attributed to the interfacial engineering between CoAl-LDH and Carbon Nitride. It was suggested that the production of midgap in the carbon vacancy modified the energy band structure enhancing photocatalytic activity [111]. Researchers are aggressively working to boost the intrinsic characteristics by incorporating active sites and generating more defect-rich structures through modifications and doping methods.

The layered morphology of LDHs exhibits efficient pathways for charge separation, minimizing the recombination of photoexcited electron hole-pairs. On integration with various semiconductors, for instance, TiO2, g-C3N4, or ZnLn2S4, LDHs develop heterojunctions creating built-in electric fields, enabling charge migration. This synergistic effect enhances the lifespan of charge carriers and elevates the reaction rate eventually enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency. A semiconductor heterostructure photocatalyst consisting of Cds particles loaded onto a nanoflower-like NiCoAl-LDH was synthesized by Ruimin et al. The developed heterostructure with strong integration of two nanomaterials with abundant active sites enabled strong interfacial interactions, facilitating the formation of an embedded electric field and elevating the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. An enhanced photocatalytic activity producing an H2 evolution rate of 2116.1 mol g−1 in 4 h was demonstrated. This strong synergistic effect among two nanomaterials CdS and NiCoAl-LDH boosted the light-harvesting ability and enhanced the interfacial charge transfer efficiency [112].

In addition to photocatalysts, the use of co-catalysts is another crucial element that must be taken into consideration as they enhance the synergistic. The goal of co-catalysts is to significantly increase photocatalytic activity by boosting the separation efficiency of photoinduced charge carriers as well as the number of active sites available for light absorption enhancement and reduction reactions. Irudayaraj developed a suitable photocatalyst to accelerate the H2 evolution activity by integrating rGO co-catalyst with NiCo-LDH. 2032.1 μmolh− 1 g− 1 of H2 production was demonstrated and it was attributed to NiCo-LDH@rGO's synergistic effect on the NICo-LDH. It significantly boosted the H2 production activity by exposing active sites and elevating the electron transport eventually maintaining the photocatalyst's stability [67]. Engineering LDHs co-catalysts can lead to a lower photocurrent onset potential, amplified active sites, faster reaction kinetics, and abridged corrosion [113]. Using LDHs as a cocatalyst, Jia Yan [16] constructed a 2D-C3N4/NiFe LDH photocatalyst. The H2 evolution rate increased significantly as a result, 14 times greater than that of 2D-C3N4 alone at 3087 μmol g−1 h−1. This enhancement is fueled by the development of a type II interface, which raises the effective surface area and promotes the efficacy of charge separation. This work proposed an optimized hole transport cocatalyst design strategy for water-splitting photocatalysts through strategic interface engineering. Wael et al. [56] utilized Ti3C2 MXene as a cocatalyst to generate a new NiAl-LDH/g-C3N4 composite. Strong interfacial contacts were produced when Ti3C2 was combined with NiAl-LDH and g-C3N4, which greatly amplified the photocatalytic H2 production action to 720 μmol g−1.

A lot of studies are reported employing LDHs as a co-catalyst as well as various other compounds as a co-catalyst with LDHs based photocatalyst. Table 4 provides the significance of employing LDHs as a co-catalyst.

LDHs are widely renowned for their exceptional stability and durability rendering them a valuable photocatalyst. The long-term operational stability of LDHs-based photocatalysts plays a vital role in their practical use in H2 production. LDHs experience photo corrosion through long-term light exposure and redox reactions that eventually degrade the material structure. Stabilization of LDHs structure with cation doping techniques like Fe, Ni, or Co and protective shell addition through carbon layers or metal oxides helps reduce photo corrosion risks [119]. LDHs experience both anion exchange within interlayers and hydroxide leaching that leads to alteration in their layered structure over a while. The stability of layers can be improved by intercalating CO3 2− or NO3 − anions while using heterostructures that combine LDHs with stable materials such as graphene oxide (GO), g-C3N4, or metal sulfides to enhance structural robustness. The active sites located within LDHs are susceptible to oxidation and poison attacks which diminish their sustained operational capability. Protective LDH layers can be formed by encapsulating them with graphene-based materials alongside conducting polymers and inert oxides to preserve both surface protection and electron transfer efficiency [67]. The incorporation of co-catalysts including Pt, Ni, Ru, CoP, and MoS2, or the design of Z-scheme heterojunctions boosts the stability of charge separation processes to improve H2 evolution performance. Stability developments in LDHs-based photocatalysts under varying pH conditions, temperatures, and electrolyte concentrations can be attained through highly crystalline LDHs synthesis with hydrophobic surface treatment. The durability and stability of LDHs-based photocatalysts for H2 generation requires essential improvement which researchers can achieve through strategies combining cation doping with heterostructure formation and co-catalyst incorporation for developing photocatalysts with permanent high efficiency.
Electronic properties play a critical role in enhancing the photocatalytic performance for H2 production. Here are some of the key electronic properties contributing to this enhancement.

Due to its distinct multi-structure and tunable band gap, LDHs are becoming more widely recognized in the photocatalysis domain. It is possible to artificially modify the molar ratio of M2+ to M3+ in host LDH laminates, adjusting their charge density and chemical characteristics to generate superior materials. Using different metal cations can greatly enhance product selectivity because they serve as active centers influencing the chemicals' selective reactions. By introducing variable valence metal ions into binary LDHs, one can adjust the valence states of these metal cations. This process boosts electron transfer and production while stabilizing highly photocatalytic active sites, ultimately enhancing the overall photocatalytic performance.

Chandra et al. [120] conducted a detailed study on metal ion substitution in LDHs and LDH/g-C3N4 hybrid composites for H2 production under visible light. They built a heterostructure with an engineered band gap and adjustable optical properties derived from the morphology of CuCdCe-LDH on exfoliated g-C3N4. This design provided a large number of surface-active sites due to the increased surface area and photostability, leading to easier charge separation and transfer. Consequently, the CuCdCe-LDH/g-C3N4 composite achieved an impressive H2 evolution rate of 3.5 mmol g−1 h−1 and an apparent quantum yield of 5.78 %.

Sufficient redox ability and a broad absorption spectrum are two of the most important requirements when constructing a conventional photocatalyst. An appropriate amount of energetic (often semiconductor) components in the support matrix would alter the electronic structure and bandgap of LDHs materials, which is advantageous for highly effective electron/hole pair separation and subsequent catalytic performance. The size of LDHs can be influenced by the ratio of trivalent to divalent cations. The divalent cations, due to their redox properties, are likely to act as active sites, while trivalent cations play a critical role in defining the overall photocatalytic performance of the LDHs catalyst. Theoretical studies have revealed that replacing Al3+ ions with electrochemically active transition metal ions reduces the band-gap energy. LDHs having bimetallic cations receive semiconducting properties via hybridization of 3D orbitals of different transition metals, upgrading their electronic characteristics. Furthermore, various combinations of transition metals consequences in variations in band gap energy, displaying the diverse synergistic interaction among the metal pairs. As an upshot LDHs with transition bimetallic cations possess reduced redox potential, extended electron life, and minimal charge carrier transfer resistance advocating a more effective oxidation or reduction process [121].

S. Megala and colleagues developed a NiAl LDH/g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 nanocomposite based on a dual direct Z-scheme to enhance photocatalytic O2 and H2 evolution. The dual direct Z-scheme photocatalysts demonstrated efficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and strong redox properties for overall water splitting. Superior H2 and O2 evolution [ 2.68 mmol g∼1 h∼1; O2 4330 mmol g∼1 h∼1] capabilities of NiAl-LDH/gC3N4/Ag3PO4 over NiAl LDH, g-C3N4, Ag3PO4, and LCN30 were validated by photoelectrochemical studies. The formation of a dual direct Z-scheme involving Ag3PO4 is responsible for the enhanced gas evolution properties of NiAl LDH, enabling more efficient and rapid separation of charge carriers [98].

The upgradation of photocatalytic activity significantly relies on the increasing number of active sites, which play a vital role in the photocatalytic process. It is crucial to note that photocatalysis occurs at these photoactive sites, inclusive of low-coordinated atoms. Research has revealed that these active sites are instrumental in enhancing charge transfer, separation, and overall photocatalytic efficiency. The amplified photoactivity of LDHs is mainly due to the stabilization of electron-hole pairs. Moreover, the integration of appropriate metal cations can adjust the energy generations of specific active sites within the catalyst as investigated using the Density Functional Theory method.

Integration of foreign metal cations into the LDHs can expand the interlayer spacing, and activate the basal planes to elevate the number of active sides, minimizing the band gap and enhancing the electron mobility of the material [122]. It can also act as an entrapment for electrons or holes and strengthen the effectiveness of charge-hole separation, which will increase the photocatalytic activity of the LDHs structure.

Bulk P–CoV-LDH serves as a precursor for n-type CoP2 semiconductors, which are crucial for enhancing H2 evolution efficiency. In addition to growing the number of active sites, the integration of CoP2 semiconductors adds a significant quantity of negatively charged phosphorous. This phosphorus captures protons and speeds up the development of H2 by acting as a base. The ZnxCd1−xS solid solution and P–CoV-LDH were successfully integrated by Xin Guo et al. in a ground-breaking study resulting in a stable and extremely effective S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst, allowing for regulated carrier migration. Interestingly, the ideal P–CoV-LDH/ZnxCd1−xS 30 % composite reached an evolving H2 rate of 1244.3 μmol without the necessity for noble metal additives, which was a 6.4-fold increase compared to ZnxCd1−xS alone. The S-scheme heterojunctions exhibited low interfacial migration resistance and exceptional efficiency in photocarrier separation [65]. Kai Wang and colleagues reported two novel tactics to boost photocatalytic H2 evolution: the synthesis of S-scheme heterojunction and the integration of an active phosphorus species. By the combination of polyhedral Ni-MOF-74 with layered CoAl LDH, these methods upgraded the electron-hole pair separation and migration while producing several active sites. The resulting Ni-MOF-74/CoAl LDH S-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst accomplished a considerably elevated H2 production rate than the initial composite chiefly due to S-scheme heterojunction. Moreover, the CoAlP/Ni2P S-scheme heterojunction exceeded the H2 production rate resulting in Ni-MOF-74/CoAl LDH revealing that phosphorous modification successfully enhances surface actives and augments the heterojunctions’ activity [102].

The production of photocurrent by LDHs serves as an essential performance indicator for H2 generation since it reflects the efficiency of both charge generation and separation. The photocurrent response is heavily influenced by electronic properties such as band structure and electrical conductivity. The bandgap width of LDHs can be modified through doping or metal composition adjustments to improve their visible light absorption. This light irradiation triggers electron transition between VB and CB within LDHs which leads to electron-hole pair formation. Hence LDHs arrange their layers in a specific manner to separate charge carriers effectively leading to decreased recombination rates. Insufficient electric conductivity in LDHs hinders efficient electron transportation to the surface where H2 production occurs [123]. Ultimately addition of transition metal doping and heterojunction formation with conductive materials and co-catalyst integration has proven to be beneficial for enhancing photocurrent production and charge carrier movement. The improved photocurrent measurements reflect better electron utilization within photocatalytic water reduction processes for H2 production when assessing LDHs-based photocatalyst systems.

An authentic approach for the evaluation of the effectiveness of charge separation and transport in an LDHs-based photocatalyst is to monitor the generation of steady photocurrent during illumination. Various LDHs-based photocatalysts such as Ni–Al LDHs display anodic photocurrent which is a characteristic of an n-type material [124]. This photocurrent depicts the effectual photogenerated separation and transportation. Wang et al. [10] reported the synthesis of NiV LDH@ZIF-67 p-n heterojunction employing the “bottle around ship technique”, depicting its efficacy as a remarkable photocatalyst for H2 evolution as well as an effective electrode for supercapacitor. The hybrid LDH@ZIF-67 structure exhibited remarkable photocatalytic H2 evolution rates, escorted by a significant enhancement in photocurrent density. The improved separation and migration of photoexcited charges, along with the efficient suppression of electron-hole pair recombination, are responsible for this breakthrough. In addition to the special p-n junction's inherent electric field, which speeds up charge transfer and further lowers electron-hole recombination, the material's exceptional H2 evolution performance was strengthened.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Significance of properties in H2 generation
ce:section.ce:para: LDHs exhibit unique structural and electronic properties making them viable photocatalysts for H2 generation. The structural properties of LDHs photocatalysts exhibit a fundamental role in H2 evolution, as they are responsible for directly influencing light absorption, charge separation, and reaction kinetics. The LDHs, which are characterized by their compartmental arrangement of positively charged metal hydroxide layers intercalated with anions and water molecules, form a high surface area that would lead to an increase in the number of active sites necessary for the aforementioned reactions. This morphological arrangement technically improves the water molecules and catalyst surface interaction, resulting in efficient H2 evolution. Furthermore, the interlayer anions, i.e. CO3 2−, NO3−, and SO4 3− typically enhanced the electronic environment and promoted charge transport eventually boosting the overall photocatalytic effectiveness. The porous anatomy of LDHs promotes reactant diffusion and better light absorption due to the augmenting photon penetration throughout the material. In addition, the oxygen vacancies, cation vacancies, and interstitial defects, particularly defect engineering as an electron trap for charge recombination suppression, thus expanding the lifespan of photon-generated charge carriers. Furthermore, the tunability of LDHs tends to form a variety of heterojunctions with other semiconductors aligning the potential energy surfaces and boosting photoinduced charge separation efficiency. Besides, the researchers by experimenting with the interlayer thickness, the spacing between layers, and the crystallinity degree, can strategically modify the size of LDHs particles to boost their performance during water-splitting reactions ultimately generating sustainable H2. The electronic properties significantly contribute towards the enhanced potential of photocatalytic H2 production by LDHs, fundamentally by the adjustments of the band structure, distribution of charge carriers, and the redox potentials. LDHs has demonstrated a broad absorption band which leads to lower visible-light absorption. However, the bandgap can be tailored through the integration of the transition metals (e.g. Ni2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Cu2+) or hetero-structures formation with narrow-bandgap semiconductors, promoting enhanced light harvesting and improved photocatalytic efficiency. Moreover, the position of conduction and VB has a pivotal role in efficient H2 evolution. The CB is required to be more negative than the H2 evolution potential (0 V vs. RHE) to allow the reduction of protons to H2 gas. The recombination of the electron-hole (e−/h+) can substantially hinder the photocatalytic activity, which is restrained by efficient charge transfer and separation. LDHs are capable of being modified to form Z-scheme heterojunctions, type-II, and other heterojunctions that expedite the directional charge transfer process enabling only the participation of high-energy charge carriers in water splitting reactions. LDHs are uncertainly capable of being a promising photocatalyst for H2 generation owing to their extraordinary electronic and structural properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scaling up hydrogen production with LDHs-Based photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The growing dependency on fossil fuels for energy generation, coupled with their adverse environmental and economic penalties, has prompted significant interest in the development of green fuel alternatives. As greenhouse gas emissions rise and fossil fuel reserves gradually deplete, governments are placing greater emphasis on environmental concerns and implementing policies to promote sustainable energy solutions. H2 is recognized as a highly adaptable energy carrier, offering substantial potential to tackle an extensive range of energy-related challenges. Researchers over the years have invented a lot of H2 production techniques. Photocatalytic H2 generation using LDHs-based photocatalysts has primarily been explored in academic laboratories, rather than being applied on an industrially relevant scale. Their application on a large scale requires a comprehensive assessment based on their sustainability and economic possibility. Table 5 provides an overview of all the existing H2 production techniques with its comparison with LDHs photocatalysis.

The raw materials required to synthesize LDHs such as magnesium, aluminum, and carbonates are widely available and abundant ensuring a reliable sustainable supply chain. LDHs offer an excellent photocatalytic material, particularly for highly desired photocatalysis powered by solar energy or visible light due to their amazing photo-responsiveness and adjustable band structures spanning from 2.2 to 3.2 eV [19]. LDHs utilize solar/visible energy for the production of H2, and significantly fewer Greenhouse gases are emitted as compared to various other methods [125]. LDHs offer a durable approach to producing H2 at a laboratory scale and are expected to provide the same enchanting results when exhibited on a large scale. The production cost of the process can be substantially reduced through optimization of the process. The economic viability of LDHs-based photocatalysts principally depends on their efficiency in renovating solar energy into H2. Higher conversion rates lead to lower production costs [126]. In generic the chemicals used for the synthesis of LDHs such as aluminum, magnesium, zinc, etc. are cost-effective when compared with other noble metals catalysts. As explained earlier LDHs-based photocatalysts exhibit excellent chemical and thermal revealing them as a sustainable source for H2 production. This minimizes the frequent use of replacement, improving the cost optimization.

Over the years, numerous scholars have synthesized LDHs-based photocatalysts intending to scale up their application for industrial use. However, despite significant progress, large-scale implementation has yet to be achieved. In 2021 Yidong [46] employed cation-exchange and precipitation methods to develop a novel 0D/3D hollow flower-like Zn0·5Cd0·5S/NiCoZn-LDH with an n-n heterojunction. 1100 μmol h−1 g−1 of H2 was generated by the photocatalyst. The work expands on the reaction process, emphasizing that Zn0·5Cd0·5S/NiCoZn-LDH establishes a type II n-n heterojunction that effectively aids in the separation and transfer of electrons and holes generated through photosynthesis, hence boosting the production of H2 by photocatalysis. This composite photocatalyst has the potential to be produced on a large scale and used in practical applications, according to the upfront synthesis procedure.

LDHs have garnered substantial attention from the scientists, keeping in regard their remarkable properties as discussed earlier. However, several factors hamper their applications and practical applications. High recombination rates between charge carriers diminish the effectiveness of HER in current systems. LDHs demonstrate insufficient electrical conductance as a baseline property which reduces effective charge movement in photocatalytic processes [127]. The operational stability of LDHs structure becomes critical since exposure to operational conditions leads to phase transitions that harm their catalytic properties. The restricted number of active sites available on LDHs surfaces limits the total catalytic activity possessed by these materials. LDHs have restricted light absorption properties mainly in the UV range as their wide bandgap hinders their ability to capture visible light which results in suboptimal use of visible solar energy. The synthesis process for LDHs encounters difficulties in achieving precise control over composition and morphology since these factors impact their photocatalytic performance [128].

While photocatalysis offers a renewable and eco-friendly approach to drive the water-splitting process under solar radiation, substantial research, and development are still required to transition this field from laboratory research to commercial application. Long-term photocatalytic reactions have shown stability issues for LDHs since they might structurally degrade while undergoing phase changes during reaction periods. Additionally, the implementation of these photocatalytic systems raises crucial concerns regarding industrial catalyst production and the consideration of energy, environmental, and economic factors. Several critical elements must be present for photocatalytic systems to be successfully commercialized, including high primary energy conversion efficiency via charge carriers, cost-effectiveness, scalability, moderate working conditions, environmental sustainability, stability, and reusability. The main factors attributing to the accessibility of LDHs-based photocatalysts are their low cost, exceptional efficiency in a variety of catalytic processes, and structural and compositional flexibility. LDHs have demonstrated significant potential as effective photocatalysts; however, their application on a large scale has yet to be fully realized.
Fig. 5 provides an illustrative overview of various LDHs-based photocatalyst applications that are widely acknowledged worldwide. Details are provided in the following sections.

Converting surplus CO2 from the atmosphere into solar fuels or useful chemicals is an alluring way to reduce pollution, climate change, and global warming. Photocatalytic CO2 conversion stands out as a promising method, utilizing sunlight, water, and a photocatalyst material. LDHs are widely being investigated for their flexible CO2 absorption ability and reasonable photocatalytic behavior [129].

Yu-fang et al. reported the synthesis of an innovative ln2S3/NiAl-LDH heterojunction photocatalyst via upfront hydrothermal techniques. ln2S3/LDH composite revealed excellent stability and photocatalytic performance in CO2 reduction in water medium. The highest methane yield, recorded after 7 h of visible light irradiation, was 36.1 μmol/(h·gcat). The 30 % In2S3/NiAl-LDH catalyst's stronger photocatalytic activity is ascribed to its higher specific surface area, better absorption of visible light, and extremely effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [130]. To improve the activity and selectivity for CO2 reduction, An-Qi et al. synthesized NiCo LDH/ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets (LDH-CN), which comprised of a Z-scheme heterojunction and a large number of active sites. The highest yield recorded of CO and CH4 were 114.24 and 26.48 μmol h−1 g−1. The heterojunction's existing electric field promoted efficient charge transfer and the separation of the photoinduced electrons and holes, which enhanced photocatalytic activity [131]. A ternary NiAl-LDH@TiO2/Ti3C2 photocatalyst was synthesized by Dong-Eun et al. exhibiting enhanced CO2 adsorption proficiencies. The incorporation of 30 wt % Ti3C2 helped this photocatalyst accomplish a prominent apparent quantum yield of 0.81 at 365 nm and unveiled a remarkable 99 % selectivity for CO over competing H2 generation. The catalyst demonstrated excellent solar-driven CO2 reduction activities. The study proved to be an innovative approach for creating an S-scheme photocatalytic system, showcasing their latent for generating energy-rich fuels through solar-driven CO2 conversion [132].

The LDHs-based photocatalysts are widely operated for degrading and adsorbing various cationic and anionic dyes. The primary forces of interaction between LDHs surface and functional groups of the organic dyes are electrostatic. In particular, the positively charged LDHs layers intermingle with anionic dyes promoting absorption on LDHs’ surface [133].

Prakash et al. reported the effective ZrRGOCuFeLDHs photocatalyst for the photodegradation of methylene blue (MB). The doped composite revealed a remarkable band gap ranging between 1.74 and 2.0 eV. The heterogeneous Zr0.6RGOCuFeLDHs accomplished an outstanding degradation efficiency of 95.2 % within 75 min of the reaction. The photocatalyst exhibited robust stability and reusability, maintaining its effectiveness over three consecutive cycles [134]. Zahra et al. by employing pyrolysis and co-precipitation techniques synthesized a Fe2O3/2D-C3N4/NiFe-LDH composite. The catalyst exhibited excellent photocatalytic efficiency proving to be a stable catalyst for degrading several cationic dyes including rhodamine B (RhB) and MB. The composite accomplished a degrading rate of 91.2 % for MB and 88.5 % for RhB [135]. Ghazal Salehi and colleagues reported the synthesis of an innovative ternary heterostructure, Mg-AlLDH@g-C3N4@Ag3PO4 intended to boost the photocatalytic efficiency through swift charge transfer processes facilitated by its discrete heterojunction design. The developed nanocomposite achieved an impressive photodegradation efficiency of 99 % for methylene blue [136].

The noteworthy physicochemical characteristics of LDHs such as the unique layered structure, immense surface area, wide range of tunable band gap, high anionic exchange capacity, wide spectrum light absorption, simplicity of synthesis, minimal cost, and high photocatalyst reusability indicate promising prospects for designing and studying the degradation of organic pollutants via photocatalysis. Mohadeseh et al. synthesized S-doped Ni–Co LDHs decorated with Fe3O4 nanoparticles and explored their photocatalytic potential. Underneath visible light, the photocatalyst effectually degraded the chlorpyrifos insecticide in an aqueous solution. In optimal conditions, the photocatalyst achieved an impressive 92.5 % degradation efficiency within 150 min. The high degradation efficiency highlighted the potential and practicality of this approach for real-world applications [137]. Guo et al. developed a heterogeneous photocatalyst reinforced by Co–Cl bonds and coated with Al-LDH/Bi12O17Cl2 to assess its potential for purifying water contaminated with persistent organic pollutants in specific environments. Under natural sunlight, the optimized Co Al-LDH/Bi12O17Cl2 composite exhibited remarkable photocatalytic efficiency, attaining 95.47 % degradation of bisphenol A and 92.47 % degradation of ciprofloxacin within 2 h [138]. CoAl-LDH/BiO(OH)xI1-x

Liyin Che with colleagues synthesized CoAl-LDH intercalated with bismuth by using a hydrothermal process for 2D/2D CoAl-LDH/BiO(OH)xI1-x heterojunction photocatalysts. The combination of/BiO(OH)xI1-x (2D) and CoAl-LDH (2D) with interlayer hydroxides contributes to enhanced heterojunction contact, light absorption, excitation, and charge carrier transport. The catalyst verified outstanding effectiveness throughout the photocatalytic deletion of organic impurities, attributed to its broad surface area and abundant OH− group. Under visible light, 10 mg/L of methyl orange decomposed completely in 20 min using 1 g/L of the catalyst, while tetracycline degraded in 5 min [139].

In recent years, antibacterial photocatalytic treatment (APCT) has arisen as a hopeful alternative to outdated antibiotics. Unlike conventional antibiotics, APCT employs a photocatalyst for water and oxygen splitting, generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as hydroxyl radicals (∗OH) and singlet oxygen (1O2). These ROS interact along with the bacterial membranes composed of proteins, lipids, or polysaccharides, leading to membrane perforation and ultimately, bacterial cell death. LDHs possessing novel 2D structure, minimum toxicity, and remarkable biocompatibility proved to a an excellent drug carrier.

Xinlong et al. industrialized a novel approach to address drug-resistant microbes by integrating LDHs with dehydroabietic acid derivatives (DHADs). The team developed DHAD/ZnAlTi-LDHs, a composite formed by the combination of DHAD that was after dehydroabietic acid achieved via a sequence of chemical reactions, along with ZnAlTi-LDHs. The study reported the dual effective functionality of DHAH/ZnAlTi-LDHs under visible light as a protective agent against UV radiation and antimicrobial action [140].

Leila and colleagues employed a templating method for developing a novel ternary hierarchical hollow assembly with CoAl- LDH nano plates integrated into the hollow carbon nitride spheres along with decorated N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs). The established composite HCNS@LDH/NCQD, under visible light, remarkably degraded tetracycline and inactivated the E. coli microbe. The boosted photocatalytic productivity was linked to two major key factors: (1) the discreet hollow structure, offering high specific surface area, enhanced active sites, and substantial light reflection gradually enhancing solar harvesting; (2) robust synergistic interaction in between the components facilitating charge carrier separation and transfer [141].

The world is steadily moving towards renewable energy sources and this makes energy storage systems crucial for harnessing renewable energy resources within the next few years. Supercapacitors are gaining noteworthy attention owing to their improved energy capacity, elevated power densities, and extended lifespan, positioning them as promising candidates for use in power management, portable electronics, and electric vehicles.

Various studies have shown the employment of LDHs photocatalysts as a significant foundation for energy storage. Guorong Wang et al. developed a NiV LDH@ZIF-67 p-n heterojunction using the "bottle around the ship" technique, indicating its effectiveness as a photocatalyst for H2 evolution and as a high-performance supercapacitor electrode. The asymmetric supercapacitor constructed with active carbon displayed a high energy density of 42.3 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 520.6 W kg−1, maintaining 81.4 % of its capacity after 5000 cycles. Moreover, it attained an extraordinary 120 % energy retention. This study highlighted the promising potential of the NiV LDH@ZIF-67 p-n heterojunction in both photocatalysis and energy storage applications [10].

Oxygen-rich CuCo-LDH@Ni2(NO3)2(OH)2 heterojunction was synthesized by Faxue et al. by employing the upfront hydrothermal and electrodeposition method. The developed CuCo-LDH@Ni2(NO3)2(OH)2 exhibited remarkable performance in energy conversion and electrochemical storage applications. Being an electrode, it accomplished high capacitances of 15.43 F cm−2 at the positive end and 5 mA cm−2 at the negative end depicting its potential in energy storage. Moreover, the electrode demonstrated exceptional photocatalytic H2 and O2 activity during the energy conversion. The team also magnificently established a novel, cost-effective, and integrated self-powered device that was skilled to concurrently produce H2 and O2 and store 2.24V energy. This study proved to be a vital advancement in multifunctional energy systems [142].

LDHs represent a highly promising class of materials, notorious for their exceptional photocatalytic performance, robust stability, and cost-effectiveness. To expand the scope of applications for LDHs-based photocatalysts, various synthesis techniques, elemental modifications, and promoter loadings have been explored. This review delves into the core aspects of LDHs as a photocatalyst for H2 evolution, comprehensively delving into its function as a photocatalyst, major structural and electrical properties, and strategies for optimizing catalytic efficiency in boosting H2 production. Furthermore, it assesses the current state of research and development surrounding LDHs-based photocatalysts at large scale. Despite considerable progress in photocatalytic H2 production using LDHs-based composites challenges remain in scaling up laboratory findings, understanding underlying mechanisms, and transitioning to real-world applications. Addressing these critical issues will be the focus of future research efforts.

Maintaining high crystallinity while avoiding agglomeration in LDHs nanosheets remains a significant challenge. However, it has been demonstrated that the crystallinity of LDHs photocatalysts can be amended by cautiously controlling the composition of trivalent and divalent metal ions. Diverse synthesis methods have been shown to produce LDHs with variable degrees of crystallinity. Future research should focus on refining synthesis techniques to attain higher crystallinity in LDHs structures. Additionally, comprehensive theoretical and analytical studies are needed to better understand and characterize the crystal structure of LDHs, providing thoughtful insights that can enhance their structural properties. Despite significant efforts to boost photocatalytic activity and develop high-performance photocatalysts appropriate for commercialization, extensive studies are required to entirely harness the photocatalytic potential of LDHs composite. There is presently a lack of comprehensive literature that explores the full functionality of each LDHs component, demanding further investigation into their specific roles. Exploring the hybridization of diverse cations could help identify the most effective combinations, with a particular focus on incorporating at least one transition metal cation to optimize performance. The ionic radii of the cations used in LDHs structures should not differ significantly, as considerable differences may lead to structural collapse. Anion selection is equally important, as it can influence the interlayer spacing and affect the adsorption of reactants, so this should be aligned with the anticipated application. Polyoxometalates, known for their semiconducting properties, could further enhance the photocatalytic capabilities of LDHs materials, making their incorporation as interlayer anions worth exploring. The actual mechanisms behind charge carrier generation and transportation still need a thorough study. Given the pivotal role of active sites in deeply influencing the photocatalytic behavior of LDHs, novel strategies are required to boost these active sites for upgraded photocatalytic efficiency.

Microwave-assisted or Hydrothermal method has the potential to appear as a cost-effective and scale technique for producing H2 at a large scale. These techniques are responsible for enhancing the crystalline structure, reducing the particle clustering, and simultaneously surging the surface area. Assimilation of innovative materials such as quantum dots, transition metal oxides as well as other carbon-based supports in blend with LDHs can remarkably enhance electron mobility and the aptitude to absorb light. Integration of hybrid systems for enhancement of photocatalytic H2 evolution by consolidation of semiconductors and support materials will prove to be a promising direction. Evaluating the commercial and environmental viability of LDHs-based photocatalysts with the existing H2 production techniques can help in further development and adoption.

Shahzer Imran: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Murid Hussain: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization.
